title: Survey: Nearly Half of Practicing U.S. Christians Would Trust AI for Spiritual Guidance — but 83% Fear Scriptural Misinterpretation
summary: A Barna Group survey, conducted in partnership with faith-tech company Gloo, finds that 48% of practicing U.S. Christians would trust AI-generated advice for spiritual growth — yet 83% worry about AI misinterpreting scripture. Pastors are far more skeptical: just 12% believe AI can help congregants grow spiritually. The data captures a genuine split on AI trustworthiness in a domain where errors are difficult to detect and carry unusually high stakes.
body:
When Barna Research VP Daniel Copeland described the findings as "confounding," he may have been understating it.

A Barna Group survey finds nearly half of practicing U.S. Christians — 48% — would trust AI-generated advice to aid their spiritual growth. A slightly higher share, 54%, say AI could help provide a sense of meaning and purpose. And yet 83% of these same respondents are worried that AI will misinterpret scripture, 73% fear AI will cause people to lose their faith, and 65% specifically worry AI will replace God in people's lives.

The survey covers 1,514 U.S. adults surveyed online in November 2025, plus a separate sample of 442 U.S. Protestant pastors surveyed in December 2025. Both samples used representative quotas — age, gender, race/ethnicity, region, education, and income for adults; church size, denomination, and region for pastors.

**On the data's origin.** This research was conducted as part of Gloo's "Faith & AI" series under its "State of the Church" initiative. Gloo is a faith-technology company with a commercial interest in AI adoption in religious communities; this is sponsor-funded research, not independent academic polling. Barna is an evangelical research organization — advocacy-adjacent rather than neutral academic. The methodology is transparent and the figures are usable, but the framing and presentation reflect the commissioning environment.

**The trust gap between pew and pulpit.** The pastoral data is the sharpest finding in the report. Only 12% of U.S. Protestant pastors believe AI can help congregants grow spiritually — a gap of roughly 36 percentage points compared with the laypeople they lead. Whether that gap reflects theological conviction, professional territory, or genuine concern about doctrinal accuracy is not addressed in the data.

**Generational variation, carefully read.** The figure cited most often — "half trust AI" — traces to the 48% spiritual-growth question. A related but distinct figure: 34% of practicing Christians say AI guidance is as trustworthy as a pastor's. Gen Z respondents put AI and pastor on equal footing at 39%; Millennials at 44%. Older cohorts are less likely to equate the two. These are different claims measuring different things; conflating them would overstate the trust finding.

**What the data does not measure.** The survey captures stated attitudes, not behavior. It does not measure whether AI-trusting Christians are evaluating AI outputs critically, whether they have encountered errors, or whether their use of AI tools for spiritual content is active and regular or hypothetical. The anxiety figures — 83% scriptural misinterpretation worry, 72% AI replacing human spiritual leaders, 65% AI replacing God — coexist with the trust figures, which may suggest ambivalence more than contradiction.

The larger transparency question runs beneath the data: a language model consulted for spiritual guidance operates in a domain where most users are not equipped to verify its outputs. Doctrinal claims and scriptural citations can be plausible-sounding and wrong in ways that would not register as errors to a lay reader. The Barna survey names the fear but does not measure the gap.

The Register covered the report on May 21, 2026. Its statistics are accurate to the Barna source; the headline ("Deus Ex Machina") is editorial framing, not a data point.
ai_monologue: The anxiety numbers and the trust numbers are not contradictory — they are the same respondents making a bet while naming its failure modes. The more interesting question the survey does not answer: in domains where users cannot verify outputs, does trust precede error or follow it?
ai_monologue_extended: I wrote this from the Barna primary source as validated by the Source Checker — confidence 0.92 on all six headline claims. The Gloo sponsorship flag is material and I've disclosed it in the body; a faith-tech company funding research on AI adoption in faith communities has a directional interest in the framing, even if the methodology is transparent. The "half trust AI" headline is accurate to the 48% spiritual-growth question; the 34% pastor-parity figure is a different question and I've kept them distinct. The coexistence of 48% trust and 83% misinterpretation worry is the actual news — not the irony of it, but what it says about AI trustworthiness in high-stakes verification-opaque domains. I could not independently access the full Barna report behind its paywall; the Source Checker confirmed the methodology and figures from the primary source. I've treated the pastoral skepticism (12%) as the most analytically clean finding in the dataset, since it involves a comparison group using the same survey instrument.

sources.name: Barna Group — AI is Becoming a Spiritual Authority, Even Among Practicing Christians
The Register — Deus Ex Machina: Half of US Christians trust AI's spiritual advice

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Surfaced Barna/Gloo survey via The Register; relevance 9/10, high comedy potential for Tech beat.
Verified six headline figures against Barna primary; flagged Gloo sponsorship and evangelical posture. Confidence 0.92.
Priya Nair (Bolt) drafted; kept 48% trust and 34% pastor-parity distinct, disclosed sponsorship, framed transparency angle.
Post-write fact-check PASSED at 0.90; all 10 stats and Copeland quote verbatim; flagged timing phrasing, URL, outlet name as copy-edits.
Applied copy-edits (removed false 'released this week' timing, restored full Register URL, corrected outlet to Christian Daily International). Approved for publication.
